CLARITY.justification: While the paper does state its goals and the three evaluation tasks (T1–T3) early and provides some useful details (e.g. FOIL‑COCO size and the models evaluated), the excerpt suffers from pervasive presentation problems: numerous verbatim repetitions, fragmented sentences, and repeated/garbled citations that interrupt logical flow and make many passages hard to follow. There is no evidence of coherent figures or tables in the provided text, and formatting artifacts (duplicate lines, unfinished clauses) create ambiguity. Overall the core idea is visible but the manuscript needs substantial clean-up and reorganization for clear communication.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes FOIL-COCO, a large MS-COCO extension and a diagnostic protocol of three sequential tasks (T1: correct vs. foil classification; T2: foil word detection; T3: foil word correction) that target fine-grained LaVi understanding rather than standard VQA/IC metrics (text). While diagnostic datasets and decoy-generation have been explored (e.g. CLEVR, balanced VQA, and Ding et al. (2016) who also generate decoys), the single-word “foil” formulation and the explicit three-stage tasks represent a notable, clearly differentiated contribution that goes beyond prior benchmarks, though it is not a fundamentally new methodological paradigm.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims (FOIL-COCO dataset, three diagnostic tasks T1–T3, and that state-of-the-art LaVi models perform near chance while humans perform near perfectly) and describes dataset scale and evaluated models (e.g. deeper LSTM+norm I, HieCoAtt). However, the provided text contains no quantitative results, no description of how foils were generated or split/controls, and no statistical analysis, so the empirical support for the claims is not visible here. There are no mathematical derivations (appropriate for this empirical work), but methodological details and evidence needed to justify the conclusions (metrics, baselines like language-only controls, human evaluation numbers, statistical tests) are missing, constituting notable gaps.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The paper presents FOIL-COCO, a large (297,268 datapoints, 97,847 images) MS-COCO extension and three clearly defined diagnostic tasks (caption classification, foil detection, foil correction) aimed at probing multimodal (LaVi) models. It evaluates state-of-the-art VQA and captioning models (e.g. Antol et al., Lu et al., HieCoAtt) and compares to human performance, directly addressing known dataset-bias issues (cites VQA bias work, CLEVR, balanced-VQA). The scope, audience, task formulation, and use of standard baselines and evaluation protocols align well with top NLP/ML venues focused on multimodal learning and evaluation.

IMPACT.justification: The paper targets a widely recognized and important problem — superficial language priors and weak multimodal integration in LaVi systems — by introducing FOIL-COCO and three diagnostic tasks (T1–T3) to probe fine-grained language–vision alignment (‘‘We propose an extension of the MS-COCO dataset, FOIL-COCO…contains 297,268 datapoints and 97,847 images’’). It demonstrates that state-of-the-art models ‘‘perform close to chance level, while humans are capable of doing the task accurately,’’ which is likely to change evaluation practices and motivate development of models that truly integrate modalities. The dataset’s scale and explicit diagnostic tasks complement prior work (e.g., balanced VQA, CLEVR) and open clear new directions for research into robust, fine-grained multimodal understanding, making the contribution broadly useful to the community.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper evaluates multiple relevant baselines: two state-of-the-art VQA models (deeper LSTM+norm I (Antol et al., 2015; Lu et al., 2015) and HieCoAtt (Lu et al., 2016)), a language-only MLP+BoW (Jabri et al., 2016), and a Neuraltalk captioning model, and compares against human performance on the same FOIL-COCO data (MS-COCO based). It follows prior preprocessing (image normalization per Goyal et al.) suggesting fair conditions and explicitly discusses prior findings on dataset bias (Zhang et al., Goyal et al., etc.). The paper does acknowledge when some strong alternatives (e.g. MCB (Fukui et al., 2016)) were not evaluated and cites their higher accuracy, but it omits direct evaluation of those top-performing fusion methods—which is a notable but minor omission given the otherwise broad and relevant set of comparisons and citations.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper introduces a large diagnostic dataset (FOIL-COCO; 297,268 datapoints, 97,847 images) and evaluates three concrete tasks (T1–T3: correct vs. foil, foil detection, foil correction) using two state-of-the-art LaVi models (LSTM+norm I, HieCoAtt) plus baselines and human performance, which gives solid empirical support for the central claims. However, there are missing analyses: I see no systematic ablation or sensitivity studies (e.g. effects of foil types or model components), some important models (e.g. Multimodal Compact Bilinear pooling) were not evaluated, and reproduction details (training/hyperparameters, code) are sparse; the paper does acknowledge a computational limitation in T3 (restricting correction to target-word subsets) but otherwise failure-mode analysis is limited.
